summarization.a: Top view of the silanol-rich amorphous silica model (A.1) containing six surface silanols (labeled 1–6) at a density of 5.07 silanols per nm².
summarization.b: Top view of the silanol-reduced amorphous silica model (A.2) containing four surface silanols (labeled 2, 4, 5, 6) at a density of 3.38 silanols per nm², plus a reactive bridge oxygen (RBO) species.
summarization.c: Top view of the idealized α-quartz model (Q.1) containing only two distinguishable geminal silanol species (labeled 1, 2) at a much higher density of 9.34 silanols per nm².

a.question: Why is it important to use amorphous silica models instead of crystalline α-quartz for studying SMI chemistry?
a.answer: Amorphous silica better represents experimental conditions and enables qualitatively different chemistry. The lower silanol density and irregular distribution on a-SiO₂ allow TB SMI configurations that cannot form on α-quartz. Additionally, α-quartz contains only geminal silanols with fixed distances, whereas a-SiO₂ features individual silanols with unique chemical environments affecting reactivity.

a.question: Do models A.1 and A.2 share the same underlying silica framework?
a.answer: Yes. Both models share the same basic framework and unit cell parameters (a = 1.072 nm, b = 1.104 nm), differing only in silanol density and RBO presence.

c.question: How does the silanol density of α-quartz (Q.1) compare to the amorphous models?
c.answer: Q.1 has 9.34 silanols per nm², which is nearly twice that of A.1 (5.07) and almost three times that of A.2 (3.38).

b.question: What is the RBO species visible in model A.2, and why is it significant?
b.answer: RBO (reactive bridge oxygen) is a strained Si−O−Si bond that reacts instantaneously with Al precursors (activation barrier <10 kJ mol⁻¹), making it a major source of unwanted growth nucleation.
